title: When Navies Block the Way: The Strait of Hormuz and Other Big Blockades
summary: Have you ever played a game where someone blocks the door so no one can get out? That's kind of what a naval blockade is — but with giant ships and whole countries! Right now, a super important waterway called the Strait of Hormuz is blocked. It's a narrow path in the ocean where about one-fifth of the world's oil and gas usually travels. The U.S. and Iran are both using their navies to stop ships, and it's causing big problems. But this isn't new. For thousands of years, countries have used blockades to cut off their enemies without sending in soldiers. One of the longest blockades is Israel's siege of Gaza. Since 2007, Israel has controlled what goes in and out by land, sea, and air. That means 2.3 million people can't travel freely, and fishermen can only go a short way from shore. Some boats have tried to break the blockade to bring supplies, but they've been stopped. Another sad blockade happened during Nigeria's civil war in the 1960s. The government blocked a region called Biafra, and about one to two million people died, mostly from hunger. In the 1960s and 70s, Britain tried to block oil from reaching Rhodesia (now Zimbabwe), but it didn't work well and cost a lot of money. The scariest blockade was in 1962, when the U.S. found out the Soviet Union was putting nuclear missiles in Cuba. President Kennedy ordered a naval "quarantine" to stop more weapons from arriving. For 13 days, the world held its breath, worried about nuclear war. Finally, the Soviets agreed to take the missiles away if the U.S. promised not to invade Cuba.
why_it_matters: When nations block trade routes, it can make fuel, food, and even toys more expensive or scarce, directly affecting your family's daily life and safety.

keywords.explanation: Using ships or soldiers to stop goods or people from moving into or out of an area.
A long-lasting blockade around a city or area to force it to surrender.
A term for blocking movement during a crisis, like the naval quarantine of Cuba.
Related to a country’s warships or navy.
A war between groups of people inside the same country.
Using energy from atoms, especially for extremely powerful bombs.

questions.question: What is a naval blockade?
options: A way to stop ships from getting through
questions.correct_answer: A way to stop ships from getting through

background_read: Gaza is a small piece of land on the Mediterranean Sea where over two million Palestinians live, and it has been under a strict blockade by Israel and Egypt for many years.
The Soviet Union (a huge country of many republics led by Russia, that existed until 1991) and the United States were fierce rivals during the Cold War, a time of great tension.
The Cuban Missile Crisis happened when the Soviet Union secretly placed nuclear missiles on Cuba, an island very close to Florida, bringing the world to the edge of nuclear conflict.
Article_Structure: WHO: Countries like the United States, Iran, Israel, Nigeria, Britain, and the Soviet Union
WHAT: A history of naval blockades, from ancient times to the current standoff over the Strait of Hormuz.
WHERE: Important global choke points like the Strait of Hormuz, Gaza, Biafra, Rhodesia, and Cuba.
WHY: Because blockades have often been used to pressure enemies without full war, but they bring great suffering to ordinary people.

perspectives.description: They believe blockades are a less bloody way to weaken an opponent and force a change in behavior without a full war.
Ordinary families often run out of food, medicine, and clean water, and many suffer or die because help cannot reach them.
They argue that blockades mostly hurt innocent people and call for corridors that allow humanitarian supplies to pass through safely.
